clusters.root_cause_summary: The stair centroid lies in a navmesh-disconnected component on the current floor: Reach_stair_centroid is False on every approach attempt across all candidates, no floor change ever occurs, and no harness-level intervention (DP tuning, permanent disable, LLM memory) can substitute for a physically navigable path to the stair.
clusters.cross_scene_pattern: Identical: Reach_stair_centroid=False on all attempts (>20 consecutive), failure tag 'never_saw_target_did_not_travel_stairs_likely_infeasible', no floor change in any candidate, all harness DPs ruled out. Different: q3zU7Yy5E5s has five distinct centroids tested across a ~0.9m region all disconnected, whereas qyAac8rV8Zk has a single centroid with oscillating min_dis_to_downstair (159–175); qyAac8rV8Zk additionally has fully exhausted intrafloor frontiers at the point the stair is first disabled, leaving zero fallback options even if the stair were cleared.
clusters.transfer_notes: T4_STAIR_FIX on qyAac8rV8Zk (candidate_3) proved that permanently clearing the stair map leads to immediate termination with zero productive fallback steps — the intrafloor frontier pool is already empty. This transfers directly to q3zU7Yy5E5s: permanent disable is equally futile there. T4_STAIR_MEM on q3zU7Yy5E5s (candidate_4) showed that LLM chain-of-thought deprioritization is not actionable when the disconnected stair is the only known cross-floor path — no connected alternative exists to nominate. Together, these two experiments exhaustively rule out all soft (memory/LLM) and hard (permanent disable) stair-management strategies; the only remaining lever is a pre-commitment navmesh connectivity query that detects infeasibility before the agent wastes its budget.
clusters.highest_leverage_untested: navmesh_connectivity_precheck_before_stair_centroid_commitment_using_pathfinder_find_path: before entering look_for_downstair or get_close_to_stair, call habitat_sim.PathFinder.find_path() from the agent's current position to the stair centroid; if geodesic distance is infinite (disconnected), skip that centroid entirely and either snap to the nearest navigable node within 0.5m via pathfinder.snap_point() or declare the episode infeasible early to preserve step budget.

clusters.root_cause_summary: The hysteresis patch introduced in candidate_2 disrupts the passive climb_stair detection path that candidate_0 used to successfully traverse stairs at step ~91–102, diverting the agent into a get_close_to_stair→look_for_downstair oscillation loop that inflates floor_reinits from 3 to 6 and exhausts the step budget; the toilet is 2D-connected and fully reachable, making this a pure iatrogenic regression.
clusters.cross_scene_pattern: Single scene. candidate_0 solves this episode cleanly (SUCCESS, DTG~0.04); candidate_2's patch changes the mode sequence from a direct passive-climb path to an oscillating three-mode cycle from step 118 onward. The failure tag 'never_saw_target_traveled_stairs_feasible' confirms the goal is reachable and the regression is behavioral, not structural.
clusters.transfer_notes: This scene is a regression canary: it demonstrates that the hysteresis patch is not globally safe — it damages episodes where passive climb_stair detection was already working. Any future candidate that re-introduces floor-switch hysteresis must explicitly preserve the passive climb_stair trigger path (i.e., hysteresis should gate only the active floor-reinit pathway, not the explore-mode stair detection that fires climb_stair organically). The contrast between candidate_0's clean fingerprint and candidate_2's oscillating fingerprint provides a precise behavioral diff for validating any revert.
clusters.highest_leverage_untested: revert_hysteresis_patch_and_preserve_passive_climb_stair_detection: revert the floor-switch hysteresis change introduced in candidate_2 to restore the passive climb_stair path; if any hysteresis is re-introduced in future candidates, scope it exclusively to the active reinit trigger and gate it behind a condition that does not intercept the explore-mode stair detection branch.

clusters.root_cause_summary: Stair traversal succeeds (floor_step resets to 0 at step 198), but the goal room on floor 2 occupies a navmesh-disconnected subgraph unreachable from the stair landing; the floor-2 frontier pool is exhausted within ~16 steps because only 2 low-scoring frontiers near the landing are reachable, and all three T4_NOQUIT rescues find an empty pool for the same structural reason.
clusters.cross_scene_pattern: Single scene. Mechanistically distinct from the pre-climb cluster: the stair IS traversed (no Reach_stair_centroid failure), but the target floor presents a partitioned navmesh where the stair landing is in one connected component and the bed room is in another. All four candidates produce byte-for-byte identical behavior (same fingerprint hash) because no intervention tested touches post-climb floor-2 frontier generation.
clusters.transfer_notes: The navmesh_pre_climb_disconnection cluster (q3zU7Yy5E5s, qyAac8rV8Zk) establishes that habitat_sim.PathFinder.find_path() reliably detects disconnection (Reach_stair_centroid=False = infinite geodesic). The same API can be applied post-climb: after floor_step resets to 0, query geodesic distance from the stair landing to each floor-2 frontier; frontiers with infinite geodesic are unreachable and should be pruned or replaced with raycasted seed points. The T4_NOQUIT rescue pattern also demonstrates that simply retrying frontier generation without changing the seed location is futile — a spatial diversification strategy (3D raycast or room-boundary detection beyond the landing) is required.
clusters.highest_leverage_untested: frontier_seeding_beyond_stair_landing_via_navmesh_subgraph_connectivity_check: after a successful stair climb (floor_step==0 transition), enumerate all navigable nodes on the new floor using PathFinder, partition them into connected components relative to the agent's landing position, and inject synthetic frontier seeds into non-landing components via the nearest boundary node or 3D raycast from the stair top — ensuring the explorer does not terminate when the landing component is exhausted but the goal component remains unexplored.

forbidden_moves.reason: Globally ruled out across all scenes per mandatory forbidden-moves list; tuning LLM response parsing has shown no effect on any failure class in this scene set.
Globally ruled out across all scenes per mandatory forbidden-moves list; frontier scoring weight does not address navmesh disconnection or stair traversal failures.

Candidate_3 on qyAac8rV8Zk proved permanent stair-map clearing causes immediate terminal 'no frontiers found' because intrafloor frontiers are already exhausted; candidate_3 on q3zU7Yy5E5s produced an identical fingerprint to candidate_0, confirming the same zero-effect outcome.

Candidate_4 on q3zU7Yy5E5s confirmed the LLM re-nominates the same disconnected centroid even with full failure history in context because no connected alternative exists; candidate_4 on qyAac8rV8Zk showed identical fingerprint to candidates 0/2 — memory injection had zero behavioral effect.
Carrot waypoint distance is irrelevant when Reach_stair_centroid=False on every attempt; the agent never enters the stair-traversal phase where DP9 would have any effect.
Floor-switch interval gating is irrelevant when no floor switch ever occurs; the failure happens before any reinit pathway is triggered.

Candidate_2 showed the hysteresis patch had byte-for-byte zero effect on both scenes (identical fingerprints to candidate_0); structural navmesh disconnection is not addressable by floor-detection hysteresis.

The hysteresis patch IS the root cause of the regression in mL8ThkuaVTM — applying it again in any form will recreate the get_close_to_stair→look_for_downstair oscillation that destroyed the previously successful passive-climb path.

Raising the floor-switch minimum interval addresses floor_confusion, which is not the failure mode here; the toilet is 2D-connected and the failure is a behavioral regression from stair-mode disruption, not premature reinit.

Stair traversal in XB4GS9ShBRE is already successful; the T4_STAIR_FIX hook is designed for failed centroid approaches and would never fire in this episode, as confirmed by candidate_3 and candidate_4 analysis.
Stair traversal succeeds, so there is no failed centroid to record; T4_STAIR_MEM is irrelevant to post-climb floor-2 disconnection.
Stair carrot distance affects the approach to and through stairs; since XB4GS9ShBRE already traverses stairs successfully, DP9 cannot affect the post-climb frontier exhaustion failure.

Four candidates with identical fingerprints confirm no harness-level DP change produces any observable behavioral difference in this episode; the failure is structural navmesh disconnection orthogonal to all 12 DPs.
